1: “The king’s heart is a stream of water in the hand of YHWH; he turns it wherever he will.” John 19:11: “you would have no authority over me at all unless it had been given you from above.” Pilate, the king-representative who sentenced Jesus, was himself an instrument turned by YHWH’s hand — the condemnation of Christ was not the triumph of human power but the execution of the divine redemptive plan. Isaiah 45:1 presents Cyrus similarly: the pagan king whose channel YHWH directed for Israel’s restoration. Every ruler who touches Christ is turned by YHWH’s hand toward the purposes of salvation.
2: Every way right in one’s own eyes, but YHWH weighs the heart. Christ as the judge who weighs the heart — John 2:24-25: “he knew all people and needed no one to bear witness about man, for he himself knew what was in man.” The divine heart-weighing of Proverbs is performed by the incarnate Christ: he sees past self-evaluation to the reality within. His Sermon on the Mount begins with “blessed are the pure in heart” (Matthew 5:8) — the heart is the arena of kingdom membership, and Christ alone can assess and cleanse it.
3: “To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable to YHWH than sacrifice.” Matthew 9:13 and Proverbs 12:7: “I desire mercy, and not sacrifice” — Christ’s repeated citation of Hosea 6:6 in his own ministry. He himself embodied this priority: his death was not the offering of a ritual sacrifice but the act of supreme justice and righteousness — satisfying the demands of justice (Romans 3:26: “so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus”). The justice that is more acceptable than sacrifice is perfectly enacted in the one act that was both justice and sacrifice simultaneously.
4: Haughty eyes and proud heart — the lamp of the wicked — are sin. Christ is the anti-type: “I am gentle and lowly in heart” (Matthew 11:29). His eyes do not look haughtily at those around him; he sees the crowd and has compassion (Matthew 9:36). The “lamp of the wicked” that is sin contrasts with Christ as the true lamp: “in him was life, and the life was the light of men” (John 1:4). The wicked lamp goes out (Proverbs 13:9; Proverbs 24:20); the light of Christ “shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it” (John 1:5).
5: Plans of the diligent lead to abundance; haste leads to poverty. Christ planned his redemption with eternal precision: the cross was not an accident or improvisation but “the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world” (Revelation 13:8). His diligent execution of the Father’s plan — step by step through ministry, suffering, death, and resurrection — produced the abundance of the new creation: “I came that they may have life and have it abundantly” (John 10:10). The diligent plans that produce abundance reach their ultimate expression in the redemptive plan perfectly executed.
6: Treasures of wickedness as fleeting vapor (hebel) and a snare of death. Against all vanishing wealth, Christ offers eternal treasure: “do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth... but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust destroys” (Matthew 6:19-20). Paul’s counter-declaration is direct: “I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord” (Philippians 3:8). The hebel-treasure of wickedness is the foil for the imperishable treasure that relationship with Christ provides.
7: The violence of the wicked sweeping them away because they refuse to do justice. Christ’s lament over Jerusalem is the compassionate counterpart: “O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!” (Matthew 23:37). The refusal of justice leads to being swept away by one’s own violence; the refusal of Christ’s mercy leads to the same eschatological consequence — the wicked swept away by the very force they chose over justice.
8: The way of the guilty is crooked; the pure man’s conduct is upright. Christ’s path was perfectly upright — Hebrews 7:26: “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners.” 1 Peter 2:22: “he committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth.” His upright conduct is both the example and the grounds of the believer’s justification: he walked the straight path that the guilty cannot walk, and his righteous conduct is reckoned to those whose path has been crooked (Romans 5:18: “one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all men”).
9: Better the housetop corner than a contentious household. Christ’s own homelessness — “foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head” (Matthew 8:20) — is the ultimate “corner of the housetop,” a chosen exposure and limitation that enabled his mission. He did not avoid the contentious household but entered it: “do not think that I have come to bring peace to the earth. I have not come to bring peace, but a sword” (Matthew 10:34) — the sword of truth that divides even households.
10: The soul of the wicked desiring evil; his neighbor finding no mercy in his eyes. Christ is the opposite at every point: he went about doing good (Acts 10:38); his eye was for the one who found no mercy elsewhere — the Samaritan woman (John 4), the tax collector Zacchaeus (Luke 19:1-10), the woman caught in adultery (John 8:1-11). Where the wicked soul seeks evil and withholds mercy, Christ seeks the lost and gives mercy extravagantly.
11: When the scoffer is punished the simple learns; when the wise man is instructed he gains knowledge. The disciples’ learning pattern tracks through both modes: they learn by witnessing Christ’s dealings with opponents (the Pharisees rebuked: Matt 23; the temple cleansed: Matt 21) and by receiving direct instruction (the Sermon on the Mount: Matt 5-7; the Farewell Discourse: John 13-17). Both modes — punitive rebuke of the scoffer and direct instruction of the teachable — are present in Christ’s pedagogy.
12: The Righteous One who watches the wicked household and throws them down to ruin. Acts 5:1-11 (Ananias and Sapphira) and Revelation 2-3 (Christ’s letters to the churches threatening judgment on the unrepentant) demonstrate the risen Christ as the Righteous One who observes and acts. His watching is not passive surveillance but active governance: “the eyes of the LORD run to and fro throughout the whole earth, to give strong support to those whose heart is blameless toward him” (2 Chronicles 16:9).
13: Closing the ear to the poor’s cry means one’s own cry will go unanswered. Matthew 25:41-46: those who refused to care for “the least of these my brothers” hear the same refusal from Christ at the judgment. James 2:13: “judgment is without mercy to one who has shown no mercy.” The mirror-principle of Proverbs 21:13 is applied by Christ to the final judgment: the same measure one uses for others is the measure used for oneself (Matthew 7:2), and the closed ear to the poor becomes a closed gate at the eschatological threshold.
14: A gift in secret averts anger; a concealed bribe averts strong wrath. The pragmatic acknowledgment of the gift-economy contrasts with the total economy of grace in Christ: “for God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son” (John 3:16). No concealed bribe or secret gift averts divine wrath — only the public, cosmic gift of the Son, given openly, bears the weight of divine anger on behalf of those who receive him. The gift that averts wrath is not secret but the most public act in history: the cross.
15: Joy to the just to do justice; terror to evildoers. Christ brings both: to his people he says “well done, good and faithful servant; enter into the joy of your master” (Matthew 25:21); to the unjust: “depart from me, you cursed” (Matthew 25:41). The same Christ who is the joy of the righteous (Revelation 19:7: “let us rejoice and exult and give him glory, for the marriage of the Lamb has come”) is the terror of evildoers (Revelation 6:16-17: “hide us from the face of him who is seated on the throne”).
16: The man wandering from understanding resting in the assembly of the dead. Christ’s mission is to seek and save those who have wandered: Luke 15:4-7; John 10:11 — “the good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.” He does not leave those who have wandered into the assembly of the dead without intervention — rather, he himself descended to death (Acts 2:27: “you will not abandon my soul to Hades”) to bring back those who had wandered there. The destination of the wanderer is the place Christ himself entered to reverse.
17: Loves pleasure comes to poverty; loves wine and oil will not be rich. Against the poverty of pleasure-love, Christ offers true riches: “I counsel you to buy from me gold refined by fire, so that you may be rich” (Revelation 3:18). Paul’s contentment-in-all-circumstances (Philippians 4:11-13) is the christological transformation of pleasure-love into pleasure-in-Christ: “I can do all things through him who strengthens me.” The poverty that follows pleasure-love is displaced by the riches of knowing Christ.
18: The wicked as ransom for the righteous. Isaiah 43:3-4 presents YHWH giving nations as ransom for Israel; here the logic runs that the wicked bear the consequences their wickedness brings, freeing the righteous from those consequences. The ultimate christological reversal is the inverse: Christ, the righteous one, became a ransom for the unrighteous: “the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Matthew 20:28). The wicked-as-ransom pattern of creaturely justice is transcended by the righteous-as-ransom of divine grace.
19: Better the desert than a contentious woman. Christ’s own experience of solitude in the desert was formative: forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11), repeated withdrawal to desolate places (Luke 5:16: “he would withdraw to desolate places and pray”). His desert withdrawals were not avoidance but engagement with the Father — transforming the desert into the place of divine communion rather than mere escape from human conflict.
20: Precious treasure and oil in the wise man’s dwelling; the fool devours it. The wise man’s stored treasure anticipates the parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30): those who invest the master’s gifts receive more; the one who buries his talent (foolishly hoards) loses even what he has. Christ is also the treasury himself: “in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3). The wise man’s dwelling that holds precious treasure is the life hidden with Christ in God (Colossians 3:3).
21: “Whoever pursues righteousness and kindness (ḥesed) will find life, righteousness, and honor.” Matthew 5:6: “blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied.” Christ himself pursued righteousness and ḥesed perfectly — “I do not seek my own glory” (John 8:50); “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death” (Philippians 2:8) — and found life in the resurrection, righteousness confirmed by the Father, and the name above every name. His pursuit of righteousness is the pattern that those who follow him are called to replicate.
22: A wise man scales the city of the mighty and brings down the stronghold they trust. Christ is the supreme conqueror of fortified strongholds. Matthew 16:18: “I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it.” 2 Corinthians 10:4: “the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds.” The wise man who scales the city of the mighty is Christ who, through the weakness of the cross, overthrew the apparently impregnable fortress of sin and death (Colossians 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame”).
23: Guard mouth and tongue — guard soul from trouble. Christ’s speech is the model: “the words that I say to you I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who dwells in me does his works” (John 14:10). James 3:2: “if anyone does not stumble in what he says, he is a perfect man.” Christ is the only perfect man who never stumbled in speech — his mouth was the perfect instrument of divine communication, guarded not by discipline alone but by complete union with the Father’s own speech.
24: The arrogant scoffer who acts with arrogant pride — the lēṣ profile. Christ confronted this profile directly in the Pharisees (Matt 23: “woe to you... for you shut the kingdom of heaven in people’s faces”) and identified its root: “you are of your father the devil, and your will is to do your father’s desires” (John 8:44). The arrogant scoffer who rejects wisdom is ultimately in the lineage of the adversary who first scorned the divine counsel. Christ, who is gentle and lowly (Matthew 11:29), is the anti-scoffing presence in the world.
25: The sluggard’s desire kills him because his hands refuse to labor. Christ’s parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30) and the servants who must work while the master is away both target the spiritual paralysis of the one who buries his gift and refuses to use it. The one-talent servant who hid his talent “from fear” — desire without action — is cast into outer darkness. Against this paralysis Christ offers both command and enablement: “work, for I am with you” (Haggai 2:4 applied in the NT frame of Philippians 2:13: “it is God who works in you”).
26: The wicked craves all day long, but the righteous gives and does not hold back. Christ is the righteous giver par excellence: “he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself” (Philippians 2:6-7). “It is more blessed to give than to receive” (Acts 20:35 — a saying of Jesus). His giving was total — his life, his Spirit, his righteousness — and he calls his people into the same pattern of open-handed generosity as the expression of the righteous character they receive from him.
27: The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination; more so when brought with evil intent. Christ’s Hosea-citation — “I desire mercy, and not sacrifice” (Matthew 9:13; Proverbs 12:7) — and his own sacrifice converge here: his sacrifice was not abominable but the fulfillment of what all acceptable sacrifices pointed toward (Hebrews 10:5-7: “sacrifices and offerings you have not desired... then I said, ‘Behold, I have come to do your will, O God’”). He abolished the category of abominable sacrifice by offering the one sacrifice of pure intent and perfect fulfillment.
28: The false witness perishes; the word of the one who hears endures. Christ is the Word that endures: “heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away” (Matthew 24:35). John 1:1: “in the beginning was the Word.” All false testimony against Christ has perished — the false witnesses at his trial are forgotten while his words endure across millennia. He is the ultimate faithful hearer who listened to the Father’s voice and spoke only what he heard (John 12:49), and his word is the one testimony that cannot be overthrown.
29: The wicked puts on a bold face; the upright gives thought to his ways. Christ’s practice of self-examination before the Father is the supreme example of thinking carefully about one’s ways: “the Father who sent me has himself borne witness about me” (John 8:18); “I always do the things that are pleasing to him” (John 8:29). His upright self-examination is continuous, transparent, and perfect — the model for the self-examination Paul calls believers to practice (1 Corinthians 11:28: “let a person examine himself”).
30: “No wisdom, no understanding, no counsel can avail against YHWH.” 1 Corinthians 1:25: “the foolishness of God is wiser than men, and the weakness of God is stronger than men.” The cross appeared to be the defeat of wisdom — “the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing” (1 Corinthians 1:18) — but it was the surpassing wisdom of God overcoming all human counsel against YHWH. The Sanhedrin’s wisdom, Rome’s power, and death itself could not avail against the YHWH who raised Christ from the dead.
31: “The horse is made ready for the day of battle, but the victory belongs to YHWH.” Revelation 17:14: “they will make war on the Lamb, and the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings, and those with him are called and chosen and faithful.” The victory-belongs-to-YHWH declaration of Proverbs 21:31 is fulfilled in Christ’s resurrection (the definitive victory) and eschatological return. All military preparation, political power, and strategic planning finally yields to the one whose victory was secured not by the horse but by the cross and empty tomb.
